paragraphs.body: By comparing Point State Park in Pittsburgh with Baitashan Park in Lanzhou, this article explores how people's preconceptions of place can be overturned by actual experience. This comparison reveals the similarities and differences between the two parks in terms of design concepts, cultural values, etc., and also reflects the different concepts of different cities in the construction of public spaces. When I first heard about Point State Park in Pittsburgh, I had a beautiful fantasy that it was a vibrant urban garden equipped with modern high-end facilities. It is located in the center of Pittsburgh, near the confluence of three rivers, which reminds me of a bustling business district full of cafes, restaurants, lively street activities and leisurely crowds. I thought that citizens and tourists here can enjoy the convenience and fast pace of urban life while experiencing rich leisure and entertainment activities. My expectations for it come from my inherent impression of urban parks, which is deeply influenced by my experience of living in Chinese cities. In my opinion, parks are not only part of the city but also part of the daily life of citizens, and should carry multiple functions such as business, social, and culture. However, when I actually arrived at Point State Park, the scene that greeted me was completely different from what I imagined. This is not a place filled with the hustle and bustle of the city, but rather a relaxing oasis — a vast and tranquil green space that has been carefully designed and maintained, surrounded by tall trees and soft lawns. The centerpiece of the park is an iconic fountain, which is located in a prominent position at the confluence of the Allegheny River, the Monongahela River, and the Ohio River. The fountain's design is simple but highly symbolic, becoming an important landmark of the city. The layout of the entire park emphasizes the harmonious coexistence of people and nature, rather than focusing too much on commercialization or urban functions. This experience made me re-examine my understanding of the concept of urban parks, and also made me realize that people's definitions of "Ideal Parks" may be very different in different cultural backgrounds. After this experience, I couldn't help but compare Point State Park with Baitashan Park in my hometown of Lanzhou. Although the two parks have different design styles and functions, they occupy extremely important geographical locations in their respective cities. Point State Park is located at the confluence of three rivers in Pittsburgh and is a core landmark of the city. Baitashan Park stands on the bank of the Yellow River and is an important landmark overlooking Lanzhou. Both parks have their own unique landmarks: the fountain of Point State Park and the white pagoda of Baitashan Park. They are not only important symbols of the city, but also carry the local history and cultural memory. In addition, the design concepts of the two are also very different. Baitashan Park is built on the mountain. The winding mountain roads and steps allow visitors to see the magnificent scenery of Lanzhou, especially the view overlooking the Yellow River and Zhongshan Bridge, which is breathtaking. A large number of traditional Chinese elements are integrated into the park, including historical buildings such as temples and white pagodas, giving it a profound cultural heritage and religious significance. In contrast, Point State Park has a flat terrain and a more modern design, emphasizing open lawns and simple spatial layout, providing citizens and tourists with a place to relax freely. Here, people can take a walk, picnic, meditate, or just sit on the grass to enjoy the sun and breeze. In addition, the differences in geographical climate also affect the greening layout of the two places. Due to climatic conditions, Baitashan Park has relatively sparse vegetation, but it makes up for its visual shortcomings with exquisite traditional buildings, while Point State Park has a mild climate, dense lawns, and shady trees, creating a more comfortable and pleasant environment. Before visiting Point State Park, I always thought that "City Center Parks" should have modern facilities and commercial functions. This concept stems from my impression of many urban parks in China — they are often not only green spaces, but also integrate multiple functions such as commercial areas, recreational facilities, and cultural pavilions. However, Point State Park, with its natural and simple design, made me realize that the existence of parks is not only to meet the functional needs of urban development, but also carries local cultural values and social concepts. Pittsburgh Park emphasizes integration with nature and tranquility, providing people with a place to rest away from the hustle and bustle; while Baitashan Park in Lanzhou is more like a continuation of culture, carrying history, tradition and spiritual beliefs. This comparison gave me a deeper understanding of how public spaces are influenced by the natural environment, social culture and urban development concepts. By comparing Point State Park and Baitashan Park, I became more aware that when facing new environments, we should keep an open mind and not judge based on existing experience alone. Every place has its own unique story, and these stories come from the local history, people and natural environment. Factors such as geographical location, climatic conditions, and cultural background jointly shape the uniqueness of each park, making them an indispensable part of urban life. This cross-cultural experience makes me cherish every unique experience and gives me a deeper understanding of cultural diversity. I am also clearly aware that every park and even every public space is a microcosm of urban and social values. What they present is not only the landscape but also the interaction between humans and nature, history, and culture. This understanding not only deepens my understanding of cultural diversity, but also makes me cherish every unique experience.
